query: What is the current state of polling and political dynamics for Zambia's August 2026 presidential election, specifically Hakainde Hichilema's expected vote share? Include recent 2025-2026 polls, opposition coalition strength, whether Edgar Lungu/PF is running (note Lungu died in 2025), and turnout factors.
answer:
I've completed comprehensive research on Zambia's August 2026 presidential election. Here's the summary of key findings:

**Election context:** Vote is 13 August 2026 under a 50%+1 threshold, with 8.79M registered voters (certified May 2026) and 14 validly nominated presidential candidates.

**Hichilema's expected vote share:** No scientific horse-race poll exists, but available evidence points to him as the clear frontrunner in the low-to-mid 50s%:
- Academic **ZERN Round 1 Survey** (Jun–Nov 2025 fieldwork): **55%** intend to vote for the president, 13% opposition, 32% undecided.
- Non-scientific online polls (even on opposition-leaning platforms) show him leading: EMV/Mwamba Facebook poll (4 Mar 2026) = ~52%; Zambian Post poll = 55%.
- 2021 baseline: he won 59.02%.

**Edgar Lungu/PF:** Lungu **died 5 June 2025** in Pretoria, and had already been ruled ineligible by the Constitutional Court in December 2024. PF is badly fractured — a government-recognized Chabinga faction, a Miles Sampa faction that defected to the ruling UPND Alliance, and the Tonse Alliance now led by **Brian Mundubile** (chosen January 2026 to replace Lungu).

**Opposition coalition strength:** Weak and fragmented across the Tonse–Pamodzi Alliance (Mundubile), People's Pact (M'membe), and Orange Alliance (Kalaba). Analysts stress this fragmentation makes it unlikely any single challenger reaches 50%+1.

**Turnout factors:** UPND regional strongholds intact on new register; risks from urban economic frustration (Lusaka/Copperbelt), below-average governance ratings, and democratic-backsliding concerns (shortened voter-roll inspection, added constituencies).

The full structured findings with all data points and sources have been submitted.

sources.title: 2026 Zambian general election
sources.snippet: General elections are due to be held in Zambia on 13 August 2026 to elect a president, members of the national assembly, councillors and council chairs.

sources.title: Tonse Alliance
sources.snippet: Formed Nov 2024; chose Edgar Lungu as chairperson/candidate. On 5 June 2025 announced Lungu's death; in January 2026 Brian Mundubile was selected as his replacement.

sources.title: Edgar Lungu
sources.snippet: Edgar Chagwa Lungu (11 November 1956 – 5 June 2025), sixth president of Zambia; died in June 2025 in Pretoria following surgery complications.

sources.title: Timeline of the Edgar Lungu presidency
sources.snippet: In December 2024 the Constitutional Court ruled Lungu's 2015–2016 partial term counted as a full term, barring him from the 2026 election.

sources.title: Main findings from the Zambia Election Research Network Round 1 Survey
sources.snippet: 55% planned to vote for the president; 13% for an opposition party; 32% didn't know/refused. 51% confident elections would be completely free and fair.

sources.title: Elections 2026: is Zambia's democratic success story beginning to fray?
sources.snippet: 26 registered candidates (14 approved); opposition fragmentation; 70 added constituencies; voter-register inspection cut from 90 to 14 days; 86% believe elections best way to choose leaders.

sources.title: Lungu's death and the perils of a weak opposition in Zambia
sources.snippet: Death of the former president on 5 June 2025 will be pivotal; without Lungu to unite a fragmented opposition, multiparty democracy will be the loser.

sources.title: HH Wins Poll on Mwamba's Page - With Majority of Votes
sources.snippet: 4 March 2026 Facebook poll: Hichilema ~52%, Mundubile ~27%, Makebi Zulu ~18%, Kalaba ~4%; over 40,000 votes; non-scientific.

sources.title: Hichilema dominates Mwamba online poll with clear 52% majority
sources.snippet: Emmanuel Mwamba Facebook poll asking 'If elections were held today, who would you vote for?' shows Hichilema at a clear majority.

sources.title: HH Wins 2026 Presidential Opinion Poll Among Top Six Popular Candidates
sources.snippet: Zambian Post Facebook poll (67,600 participants) projected Hichilema to secure 55% of the vote.

sources.title: 8.7 Million Zambians Certified to Vote in August Election
sources.snippet: ECZ certified 8,786,300 eligible voters for the 13 August 2026 general election after removing duplicate and deceased records.

sources.title: Zambia certifies 8.79 million voters for August 2026; women account for 53.1%
sources.snippet: 8,786,300 eligible voters; provisional register 8,888,912; mass registration Oct 13–Nov 29, 2025.

sources.title: Alliance politics take centre stage as Zambia heads to August polls
sources.snippet: 14 candidates cleared; UPND Alliance expanded to 15 parties (incl. Miles Sampa PF faction); Tonse–Pamodzi under Mundubile; People's Pact (M'membe); Orange Alliance (Kalaba); NA expanded 156→226 seats.

sources.title: 14 Presidential Candidates Validly Nominated for 2026 Poll
sources.snippet: ECZ validly nominated 14 presidential candidates and running mates, including Hichilema/Nalumango (UPND).

sources.title: Opposition Fighting One Another, Destroying Prospects for an Opposition Win in 2026 - Emmanuel Mwamba
sources.snippet: Hichilema accused of infiltrating/hijacking the PF via Robert Chabinga and using the courts to bar Lungu.

sources.title: Patriotic Front (Zambia)
sources.snippet: PF founded 2001 by Michael Sata; led by Lungu after 2014; lost power to UPND in 2021; supported by youth and urban poor.

sources.title: Zambia 2026: Can Lungu revive a divided opposition to challenge Hichilema?
sources.snippet: Analysis of divided Zambian opposition and Hichilema's economic reforms ahead of 2026.

sources.title: Zambia: The Challenges of Maintaining Democratic Progress
sources.snippet: Hichilema seeking second term; won 59% in 2021 with strong youth turnout; country of 22 million.

sources.title: Zambia's 2026 election: How Hichilema is tilting the playing field against opponents
sources.snippet: Cites electoral irregularities, takeover of main opposition party, and police used to block opposition assemblies.
